142 THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.

- 1805 edition raise a question as to the accuracy of Whitelaw and Cotqp

in -alleging that ‘‘ Psyche’ was printed at Rosanna. It is, as readerg
know, a very long poem and there was no reason why it should b
printed there. All the same the statement in Whitelaw is distinet jy
its averment of the printing there and inasmuch as Whitelaw’s history
appeared in 1810, shortly after Mrs. Tighe's death, one 1s inclined tq
think that he must have had some reason for his allegation and he ig
hardly likely to have been entirely misinformed.

There would seem to have heen some reason for supposing that
there had been some-poem or poems of Mrs. Tighe’s printed 1n Rosanna.,
Now Mr. Strickland, of the National Gallery, Dublin, has very kindly
afforded me an opportunity of examining a little booklet, 16mo., en-

titled ‘‘ Mary, a series of reflections during twenty years.” It beams

the date on the title-page ‘“1811,”” but has no imprint whatever. Thesa
selections are short poems by Mrs. Tighe and fill 31 pp. The type, the
paper, the style and the general ‘‘get up’’ of the little booklet cer-

| tamﬂy suggest that it came from a private press. It has a brown paper

cover and page 14 is blank, as is also page 32. There are some MS.
notes throug-h the booklet and at either end, and some blank leaves are

~inserted beuween pp 14 and 15. In addition there are some printed prose

paragraphs in it referring to Mrs. Tighe’s death and other matter. She
had died in the previous year. This little booklet may have been printed

‘at Rosanna, or in the Co. Wicklow, and, containing some of Mrs. Tighe's
: poems, may have given rise to the story that her poem ° Psyche’’ was
printed there. I think it is hardly likely that Psyche’’ could haveé

been privately printed after 1805, the date of the edition which Was

ndoub’oedly printed in London and is the first edltlon known. I have

o | _'not geen any. mentlon of a reference to this little booklet anywhere, Dor
e ~do I know of any copy save the one which Mr. Strickland has. There
3 -,'-.}'»15 an MS. -note in it that it was to be shown to nobody but ‘those in-
,tere.sted in the subject. Then arises the question how did the printing
B press come to Rosanna? -How long was it there? Was anything elso

; -'~"'_‘_-'-"pr1n'bed at it? Rosanna‘'is in the Co. ‘Wicklow, not - very far from
S Rat.hnefw Railway Station, near the Devil’s Glen, a famous beauty spoY

d. Now

THE WILSON CROKER COLLECTION.

Half a century after the death of this well-known politician and
man of letters his librany is being sold by Mr. Thorpe, of St. Martin’s
Lane, London. So perhaps some notes.on its Irish items may prove
interesting. But first, of the man himself. Born in Galway in 1780,
brought up in Cork, educated at Portarlington and T.C.D., where he
was contemporary with Moore, he early showed an indination to litera-
ture and his ‘‘ I"amiliar Epistles to F. E. Jones on the Irish Stage,”
1805, ran through twenty editions. Elected M.P. for Downpatrick by
three votes under circumstances detailed in Daunt’s Eig]ity-ﬁve: years
of Irish History ”’ (Downey, 1888), he rapidly made a name for himself

in_politics—replying to Grattan the night he took his seat. He was ‘

appointed Secretary of the Admiralty, a post he held for over twenty
years which enabled him to wssist Crofton Croker when left fa,therless.'
One of the founders of the ‘‘ Quarterly Review,”” he continued a con-
tributor for nearly half a century. The savagery of his criticism and the

-venom with which he attacked Lady Morgan and other political oppo-

nents made him greatly disliked, though he numbered amongst his
friends Scott, Southey, Canning and We]hngton Disraeli has satirized

“him in ““ Coningsby > as Mr. Rlgby and Thackeray delineated h.lm as

Wenham in *‘ Pendennis.” No life of him has been wrltben,_ but his

““Correspondence ”’ was pubhshed in 3 vols. in 1884 (Murray), and- sheds:
~many sidelights upon the politics and: literature of the first half of. t.he e
Past century. A voluminous writer and an ardent, collector, his ummral- e
| led collection, of pamphlets on the French Revolution was purcha-sed by i Ty
 the British Museum shortly after his death, and now the general collec- .~ ¢
- tion comes on the market. Many of the books are enrlched 'mth mar- ,( -_ e
- 8mal annotations and many are ppesent'itmn copies such as Banim’s
“Celt’s Paradise,” and ‘‘ Miscellaneous Sketches M of Arthur Browne,
'_me'mbe'n for the Universityt—*‘ withy his sincere- 1~espects 2 Ra,t]:m 8 |
- compliment to a lad of i ghteen There are innumerable Union- Ta‘acts,* i '

1800-30. Oneo ent;tled £ Orokenana,” Dublin, 1818; is-a satlre upon hl_m--,f‘

Answered,”’ 1820. There is a copy of his own poem *‘ Talavera,” 9th e

and the printed-catalogue ascribes to him a rare anonymous pamphlet
- on the Emmet outhreak—** The opinion of an Impartial Observer,” 1803,

The - MS. catalogue of the library is contained in two small 4to vols,

whilst his entire contributions to the ** Quarterly—savage and tartarly”
- from 1812 tall 1850, are contained im mine volumes bound 1in green
~morocco. I reserve a notice of the MSS. until next month.

“ THE STRABANE MAGAZINE.”

Of all the Irish provincial periodicals that saw the light during

~ the eighteenth century, this must be one of the scarcest. A keen
- search extending over a dozen years has resulted in unearthing only

-f—w_o' copies, one, much dilapidated, owned by Mr. D. McAnaw, of Stra-

_bane, and another which lacks a title-page—if one ever existed—and
~the last leaf, in the possession of the editor of I.B.L. FFrom the latter
: copy I ‘béke;the following particulars which somesrhat modify- the state-
-__ipents in- my-‘“Notes on the Literary History of Strabane,” Omagh,
, :190 Thé volume vcontainls twelve monthly numbers covering the whole
;year 1800, each mumber containing 48 pp., measuring 8} x 5. There
'-“;'.d'lrs no indication of its price, or where or by whom printed, but internal
,._Tewdence points to Strabane, where printing had flourished for thirty
-'3-:_7’13~ears prewously For instance in the first numben there is an-obituary
* “notice of Dr. Crawford (died 4th January), the historian who had ve-
"'_‘31ded there for thirty years, and whose good work ‘‘ entitles him to the
'.'affecmona,te remembrance of the Inlmbltants of this Town.”” The date
; quot-ed wnth several others, show that the magazine appeared about
- - the mlddle of the month. Tt originally hore the title of *“ The New
; Magazme 73 'bhrough a letter in the third issue is addressed *¢To the.

Edltor of the 6trabane Magazmre,’_’ a title- which it did not assume

untll the 7th: or. 1) uly number. The first number contains inter alia an

eulogmm on Lord Cha.r]emont an account of the Cathedrals of Ireland,
ther death anxl funeral of Waslnngton and a2 poem on ‘‘ The Plain of

E] the prayer was doubtless inserted from the copies of the Holy Week S 1 |
Office issued prior to 1763. The earliest Dublin copy of the Holy Week ' '.1’1 ; 1

'[ Office I have seen is that issued in 1770 by Richard IFitzsimons at the | R
i King's Head, High Street. Probably the prayer was continued in the ; * 4| 3

subsequent editions until aften the death of King Charles III., who | ‘i
died January 3lst, 1788. After the year 1790 the prayer was altered to R
the King, meaning King George IILL. W. . H..G.”F-

g DerMo’p O’Conxor (Vol. I1I., p. 127.)—From notes supplied to me = '

by the late Maurice Lenihan of Llnli'l ick in 1881, I learn that Dermo’d = ~fe
0’Connor was the =on of Tadhg roe O’Connor of Limerick and was by -~ },
5{. profession: a heraldic painter. He took a keen interest in Irish history, = 1
‘ antiquities and music, of which latter he was an early, if not the first, e '

collector on record. In 1726 he noted down a number of beautiful Irish
airs for Daniel Wright of London, ‘‘ Next the Sun Tavern,’” which
were published by Wright in the following year in a work entitled,

““ Aria, di Camera,” his name being printed ‘“ Mr. Dermo’t O’Connor
of Limerick.’”” So scarce is this publication—a 12mo. that only one copy
can be traced, viz., that in the possession of Mr. Frank Kidson of
Leeds. T have seen it sfated that O’Connon was alive in 1732, but Mr.
